origin: I grew up watching my dad fix things around the house and always liked working with my hands. In high school I took a vocational trades elective and realized I was more interested in the trades than a four-year degree. I looked into apprenticeship programs, found the UA Local 26 program in Tacoma, and enrolled in the pre-apprenticeship at Clover Park before getting accepted. I worked at Home Depot in the plumbing department while I was in school, which gave me product knowledge before I ever touched a job site.
why_plumbing: I like that every job is a real problem with a real solution. When a customer has no hot water or a restaurant is shut down because of a drain backup, you show up, figure it out, and fix it. There is a clear end to the work and you can see what you did. I also liked that the apprenticeship meant I was earning while I was learning — I was not taking on debt to train for a job, I was getting paid to train for it.
career_direction: I want to get my master plumber license within the next two years. After that I am open to either staying in the field on larger commercial projects or eventually running a small service company. I am not in a rush to get off the tools — I still enjoy the work and I think the best plumbing contractors are the ones who spent real time in the field before they started managing others.
differentiators: I have a backflow prevention tester certification that most journeymen at my level do not bother to get — it opens up work that regular plumbers cannot legally perform and makes me more useful on commercial accounts. I also have crew lead experience from the mixed-use project that most journeymen my age do not have yet. And my customer ratings are something I take seriously — a 4.9 across 60 reviews in a service business is not an accident, it comes from actually communicating with people and showing up when you say you will.
what_i_value_in_work: A company that does the work right the first time and does not cut corners on materials or code compliance. I have worked for contractors who would rather argue with an inspector than fix the problem and it creates more work for everyone. I want to work somewhere where the foremen have high standards and the apprentices are actually being trained, not just used as laborers.
problem_solving: I start by isolating the problem before touching anything. A lot of callbacks in plumbing happen because someone fixed the symptom and not the cause — a drain is slow, they snake it, it backs up again in two weeks because nobody looked at the grade on the line. I try to understand what is actually happening before I start taking things apart. When something is unfamiliar I ask the older guys before I guess — there is no shortage of people in this trade who have seen the same problem before.
weaknesses: I can be impatient when I think a job is being done the wrong way and I do not always wait for the right moment to say something. I have had to learn to pick my battles on job sites and bring things up to a foreman privately instead of in front of the crew. I am also still working on my estimating — I can do a material takeoff but I tend to underestimate labor hours on unfamiliar work.
what_energizes_me: Getting a clean rough-in done on a complicated floor plan where the trades are stacked on top of each other and everything fits. Or diagnosing a problem that stumped somebody else — a slab leak that turned out to be a pinhole in a spot nobody checked, or a pressure issue that was coming from a failing PRV that had been blamed on the city main for two years. The common thread is figuring something out that was not obvious.
key_narrative_origin: The apprenticeship decision. Most of my friends went to four-year schools and came out with debt and jobs that were fine but not what they expected. I went into a trade, got paid to learn, came out with a license, and had no debt. I do not think it is the right choice for everyone but it was clearly the right choice for me and I would make the same decision again.
key_narrative_conflict: On the mixed-use project the original riser design had a conflict with a structural beam that the engineer had not caught. The GC wanted us to just notch around it without getting an engineer to sign off. I told the foreman I was not comfortable doing that without approval because it was a load-bearing member and we would own the liability if anything happened later. It held things up for two days while we got a revised detail from the structural engineer. The GC was annoyed but the foreman backed me up. The revised detail actually improved the routing anyway.
